name: HUSK
description: Self-hosted AI memory server with vector search, knowledge graph, session compression, and multi-user support. All your machines connect to one instance.
tagline: AI memory server with search and session tracking
approachDetail: Vector search via Qdrant or sqlite-vec; knowledge graph with edges (caused_by, contradicts, supersedes, related_to) via SQLite; 5 embedding backends (transformers, ollama, openai, voyage, llamacpp)
storageDetail: sqlite-vec for zero-dependency local vector search; Qdrant for production deployments; SQLite for graph edges and metadata
scopesDetail: Memories scoped to session, project, or global with independent TTL defaults per scope
duplicateDetail: Cosine similarity threshold, configurable per scope
retentionDetail: Each scope has default TTL, overridable per memory. Expired memories are garbage collected
multiUserDetail: Create users, generate API keys, invite a GitHub org
crossMachineDetail: Self-hosted server — all your machines connect to one instance
webUiDetail: Admin dashboard for users, settings, and memory management
tokenBudgetingDetail: Pass max_tokens to retrieval — server returns as many memories as fit
contextInjectionDetail: Session-start hook auto-injects recent session summaries as additionalContext; configurable count (default 5, max 20)
networkDetail: Fully offline with sqlite-vec + transformers (model cached after first download); Qdrant + Ollama path needs those services running
auditTrailDetail: Session observations table tracks every user prompt, tool use, and file modification with timestamps
pros: Session compression with multiple LLM providers
cons: Runs as a local server process (lightweight with sqlite-vec, heavier with Qdrant)
Neo4j graph backend is stub-only (SQLite graph works)
